title: Ritul's Tabs
narrative:
Everyone loves keeping tabs. Some have 10 and others have 100s. Ritul loves his tabs the most, but he has waaaaay too many of them open at one time, so he's going to close some to help him decide whether to work or to chill.

He decided on a simple method: close every k-th tab. Formally, Ritul will choose one tab (let its number be b) and then close all tabs with numbers c = b + i·k that satisfy 1 ≤ c ≤ n, where i is any integer (positive, negative, or zero).

For example, if k=3, n=14 and Ritul chooses b=8, he will close tabs 2, 5, 8, 11 and 14.

After closing the tabs, Ritul will count the remaining study tabs (e) and the remaining music tabs (s). Help Ritul calculate the maximum possible absolute difference |e − s| over all choices of b — so he can easily decide what to do next.
input_spec:
x is a 2-element list:
  x[0]: an integer k (2 ≤ k < n)
  x[1]: a list of n integers, each 1 or −1. The i-th value is 1 if tab i is a study tab, −1 if it is a music tab.

Constraints: 2 ≤ k < n ≤ 100.
output_spec: Return the maximum absolute difference |e − s| over all valid choices of starting tab b.

examples.note: Choose b=1 or b=3. One tab of each type is removed, leaving e=2, s=0. |e−s|=2.
Optimal b leaves only music tabs open.

hints: The closed tabs are exactly those whose 0-based index satisfies `index mod k = (b-1) mod k`. So you're choosing one residue class mod k to remove.
There are only k residue classes to try (0 through k-1). For each, compute the sum of tabs removed, then the remaining sum = total_sum - removed_sum.
You can compute the sum per residue class without a loop: `(til count tabs) mod k` gives the class of each tab. Then group or accumulate.
Try: `s:k#0; s[(til count t)mod k]+:t` — this accumulates the tab values into their residue bucket in one pass.
The remaining sum after removing class i is `sum[t] - s[i]`. You want `max abs sum[t]-s`.
winning_criteria: FASTEST correct single-parameter func wins. If within 10% of the fastest timing, shortest code wins. Measured with `\t:1000 func x` (single call, not each) and `-2+count string func`.
